topic: Oxysdonsaurus — exists only as a misspelled name and a single tooth
what: Oxysdonsaurus is an extinct genus of crocodylid crocodilian from the Oligocene, known from the Paraná Basin in Argentina. It is known only from a single tooth and is considered indeterminate. Its name is a misspelling — Edward Drinker Cope intended 'Oxyodontosaurus' but wrote it wrong, and the error became the official name.
why_interesting: The thinnest possible thread of existence in the fossil record: one tooth, probably misidentified at the genus level, named by accident wrong. Oxysdonsaurus is real in the taxonomic sense — it has a name, a page, a place in the tree of life — but it's almost nothing. It's a placeholder for something we can barely prove existed. And the name is wrong. The creature, whatever it was, has been immortalized under a typo.

connections.note: Cope intended Oxyodontosaurus; the error became the accepted name

uncertainty: How many other genera exist in the same state — known only from a single fragmentary specimen, considered indeterminate, essentially ghosts? Is there a name for this category of existence in taxonomy?
